bullets: Reinsurer can assist with capital strain at product launch — particularly where large indemnity commission is payable at inception.
Financial reinsurance: reinsurer capitalises future surplus flows at later policy durations to provide up-front lump sums to offset launch strain.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance primarily used to manage capital and cashflow rather than to transfer insurance risk; the reinsurer capitalises future surplus flows to provide upfront lump sums to offset new business capital strain.
The shortfall arising at policy issue when the sum of initial expenses, required reserves and solvency capital exceeds the first premium received; funded from the insurer's capital.

subsections.title: Technical Assistance
bullets: Reinsurer can help with new launch or product redesign via: market surveys and customer opinions; product design and wording; pricing data; medical underwriting forms and wording; direct expert input into insurer processes (e.g. secondment of staff); risk sharing and design of treaties; background on regulatory issues.
Reinsurers are in contact with many insurers and share collected information where it does not betray confidentiality or infringe competition legislation.

subsections.title: Overview
bullets: Investment policy affects product design and pricing — deciding on suitable investment strategy should be part of the design process itself.

Main aim: (a) select investments appropriate to nature, term and currency of liabilities; (b) maximise overall return (income + capital gains); (c) extent to which appropriate investments may be departed from to maximise overall return depends on extent of free assets and risk appetite.
Investment policy is directly linked to the investment return assumption in the pricing basis.

subsections.title: Maximise Return
bullets: Rate chosen depends on return from assets in which reserves are invested — determined by asset-liability modelling exercise.

Health insurance products typically invest in fixed-interest assets/bonds; unit-linked or pre-funded LTCI (long investment period, need to mirror care cost increases) may use equity-based approach or index-linked assets.

Opportunities to mismatch to improve investment returns depend on: risk appetite of managers/owners; availability of free capital; extent to which portfolio is growing (income exceeding outgo creates short-term investment opportunities).

subsections.title: Asset-Liability Matching
bullets: Assets chosen with regard to nature of contract — whether benefits are fixed in monetary terms, terms of the policy, and currency.
Perfect matching is a theoretical ideal; allowance for mismatching should be made either as a margin in the assumed investment return or as an increased allowance for risk in the risk discount rate.

key_terms.definition: Investment strategy of selecting assets whose cashflow characteristics (amount, term, currency) mirror those of the liabilities, minimising investment risk.
A margin in the investment return assumption (or increase in the risk discount rate) to reflect the additional risk taken on when assets do not perfectly match liabilities.

subsections.title: Type of Investment
bullets: Amount, term, type and currency of investment must reflect product design — currency mismatching is undesirable (e.g. sterling products should not be backed by foreign-currency assets).

subsections.title: Liquidity
bullets: High liquidity required for PMI — encourages use of cash or quickly convertible investments.

Growing PMI portfolio requires net new policies each year (excess new policies over lapses) — reserves increasing, requiring capital injections as renewal income is at lower level than needed to subsidise first-year costs.
Assets backing PMI portfolio may need to be disinvested when outgo exceeds income — most likely when claims are heavier than expected or when fund is in decline (lapses exceed new business).
Return may be sacrificed to some extent to satisfy liquidity needs — short-term investments or cash used in such circumstances.

key_terms.definition: The ease with which assets can be converted to cash to meet short-term liabilities; high liquidity is particularly important for PMI due to its high claim frequency.

subsections.title: Consistency with Inflation
bullets: Investment return assumption must be consistent with inflation assumptions — relationship between assumed investment return and inflation should be realistic.
Four inflation variables, each potentially at different rates: claims cost inflation; premium inflation; administration expense inflation; claims expense inflation.

PMI claims inflation is complex: (i) increased policy utilisation (higher claim frequency); (ii) technology upgrades (more expensive procedures/equipment/drugs); (iii) age-related cost increases (older policyholders cost more to treat); (iv) like-for-like procedural cost increases.
PMI claims inflation drivers: supply costs, resource availability, demand for services, deals between insurers and hospitals — resultant rate may differ widely from retail prices or average earnings.
Expense inflation contributors: ordinary expenses driven by wage inflation and IT costs; claims administration expenses driven by wage inflation and specialist medical reports/tests costs.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which healthcare costs increase over time; driven by increased policy utilisation, technology upgrades, age-related cost increases and like-for-like procedural cost increases — may differ significantly from retail price or average earnings inflation.

The rate at which operating expenses increase over time; ordinary expense inflation driven by wage inflation and IT costs; claims expense inflation driven by wage inflation and cost of specialist medical reports and tests.

subsections.title: Short Term or Long Term
bullets: PMI is short-term — investment return has minimal impact; inflation of claims and expenses can be incorporated into premium at renewal; repricing can be carried out yearly in response to experience.
CI, IP and LTCI are long-term — investment return is more significant (especially for pre-funded LTCI where reserves are substantial).
Longer guarantee periods require larger margins for uncertainty in investment and inflation assumptions.

Inflation assumptions are crucial for long-term products, though reviewable premium basis provides some opportunity to reset assumptions at review (limited by TCF obligations and competitive constraints).

subsections.title: Renewability
bullets: In some territories, health insurers give policyholders guaranteed right to renew (may only apply to certain products).

Long-term policies (UK: CI, IP, LTCI — minimum potential 5-year term): insurer cannot cancel cover; must offer cover for entire policy term; rates may be reviewed; policy conditions cannot be changed. Referred to as non-cancellable.
Some countries allow conditionally renewable business — circumstances in which insurer can refuse renewal are specified.
Short-term policies (PMI): terms on renewal may be identical to those for a new entrant with same risk characteristics. Policyholder must notify insurer of changes in circumstances.
Short-term renewal premium may alternatively reflect experience in previous period via full experience rating (groups) or NCD (individual).
Insurer can change product conditions at short-term renewal (effectively withdrawing old plan and offering new one) or even cancel cover at renewal date.
Renewal rates significantly affect pricing — initial expenses must be spread appropriately over expected policy 'lifetime'.

Rate schedules may attract a mix of risks different from that expected — actuary must be aware of all policyholder classes to whom renewal premiums might apply and iteratively adjust rate levels/applicability.

key_terms.definition: Describes a long-term health insurance policy that the insurer cannot cancel during the policy term; cover must be provided for the entire term regardless of the policyholder's health status or claims experience.
Describes business where the insurer can refuse renewal only under specified circumstances as set out in the policy conditions.
Premium adjustment at renewal based on the actual claims experience of the group in previous period(s); commonly used in group PMI.

No Claims Discount — a premium reduction given to policyholders who have not claimed in a specified period; acts as a proxy for risk segmentation and operates similarly to an excess by creating a financial deterrent against small claims.

exam_qa.q: State a second possible advantage of NCD and experience rating systems (beyond reducing incidence of small claims).
exam_qa.a: Policyholders should be less likely to lapse their policies, as they may believe they would not get as good a deal on switching to a rival insurance company.

subsections.title: Reviewability
bullets: Reviewability: degree to which premiums, guaranteed renewable under long-term contracts, can be changed in amount at insurer's discretion.

All health and care insurance policies are subject to future experience uncertainty; insurer retains flexibility in benefits/premiums to keep cover appropriate and premiums consistent with original pricing targets.
Reviewable premiums allow insurer to alter premiums if portfolio claims experience differs from originally expected — typically reviewed every 5 years, with ongoing experience monitoring.
Reviewability relates to changes in claims experience of the whole portfolio — not to individual policies with bad claims history.
Guaranteed premium rates also offered, but at a premium to reviewable rates.

Reviews of premiums can result in 'selective lapsing' — healthy lives lapse (or accept reduced benefits) while those claiming/in poor health agree to increase. Some insurers restrict increases to a maximum percentage per year to reduce selective lapsing.

Where experience is better than expected, some insurers reduce premiums — this is communicated from the outset to set expectations, reducing selective lapsing and potentially allowing lower margins in pricing basis.
Two design extremes: full guarantee of future premiums (high guarantee loading) vs no guarantee whatsoever. Middle ground: guaranteed for first 5 years, fully reviewable thereafter.
A fully reviewable product is difficult to review in practice because of market pressures — bad experience alone is not usually good enough reason to justify a rate increase.

key_terms.definition: The degree to which premiums, guaranteed renewable under long-term contracts, can be changed in amount at the insurer's discretion, typically in response to changes in portfolio-wide claims experience.

The tendency for healthier, lower-risk policyholders to lapse their policies (e.g. in response to premium increases), leaving a worse-than-average risk portfolio and worsening future claims experience.

Additional premium charged on guaranteed-rate policies (versus reviewable-rate policies) to compensate the insurer for removing the insurer's right to increase premiums in response to adverse experience.

subsections.title: Example of a Statement of Good Practice
bullets: In some jurisdictions, statements of good practice outline how an insurer can review certain classes of business.
UK example (2005): co-operation between FSA, UK Ombudsman and ABI produced a Statement of Good Practice for reviewable long-term protection policies.

Consumer benefits: clearer explanations of reviewability; greater confidence that review increases/decreases are calculated fairly; continued availability of reviewable products; ability to buy protection at lower cost than guaranteed rates.

Practical implications for insurers: prominently show reviewability in product literature; base reviewable premiums on assumptions believed valid for full policy term; review assumptions regularly; use consistent assumptions for in-force and new business; increase premiums at review only for 'valid reasons' as defined; not aim to recoup earlier losses at reviews; notify customers of review results; consider option to reduce cover instead of paying higher premium; keep records of assumptions and analyses supporting reviews.

key_terms.definition: A document produced in some jurisdictions (e.g. UK 2005, produced by FSA, UK Ombudsman and ABI) outlining how insurers should conduct premium reviews of reviewable long-term protection policies.
Association of British Insurers — the UK insurance industry trade association; issues Statements of Best Practice on policy wording and industry standards.
Financial Services Authority — the former UK financial services regulator (now replaced by FCA and PRA); co-produced the 2005 Statement of Good Practice on reviewable premiums.

subsections.title: Increase Options
bullets: Increase options allow cover to be increased without further evidence of health (guaranteed renewability with increments).

Type 1: increases incorporated in original contract, operating automatically — at fixed rate (e.g. 3% pa) or linked to external index (e.g. RPI); premiums may be level, increase in line with benefit, or increase by other specified pattern; alternatively premiums are costed as they arise (costed increases start from a lower initial level but rise more steeply than pre-ordained increases).

Type 2: periodic option for policyholder to increase benefit by fixed percentage via new policy costed at then-current age and outstanding term; no re-underwriting (policyholder not re-examined) creating adverse selection — loading must be included in original premium basis to cover this option cost.

Risk of Type 2: if revised product design leads to higher sickness benefit value than anticipated, original loading was inadequate. Also, policyholders may be disappointed if unable to increase benefit in the way they expected, harming reputation and increasing lapses.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the policyholder to increase the level of benefit at specified intervals without providing further evidence of health (i.e. without re-underwriting).
Increase option Type 1 where premium increases are charged as they arise (costed at then-current age and term); starts from a lower initial premium but rises more steeply than pre-ordained increases.
Increase option Type 1 where benefit increases and corresponding premium increases are fixed in advance in the original contract; initial premium is higher but increases are predictable.
Increase option incorporated in the original contract and operating automatically, either at a pre-ordained rate (fixed % or index-linked) with pre-set premiums, or costed as they arise.

Increase option giving the policyholder a periodic right to increase benefit by a fixed percentage via a new policy costed at then-current age and term, without re-underwriting; adverse selection loading required in original premium basis.

subsections.title: Pricing Guarantees and Options
bullets: Pricing a guaranteed product must reflect likely changes in experience over policy term — difficult when claims experience is changing.
Main driver for switching to reviewable premiums: significant increases in reinsurer rates for guaranteed premiums (some reinsurers stopped offering guaranteed rates on certain products).
Guarantee considerations also apply to products with level premiums throughout or where benefit increases are incorporated in the original contract.
Additional margin required for guaranteed products to allow for unpredictability of future benefit increases.

subsections.title: Converting the Form of Benefits
bullets: Long-term health/care products focus on income (IP, LTCI) or lump sum (CI); any can be designed to encompass either; at claim, circumstances may make claimant wish to take payment in the other form (partially or entirely).

Converting capital to income (CI): CI insurer may use part/all of lump sum to purchase purchased life annuity (PLA) — guaranteed income for remainder of lifetime or fixed period. Customer disadvantage: income level unknown until claim paid (depends on age, term, annuity rates, health at claim). If annuity follows CI claim, impaired life annuity may be sought — market is specialised.
Converting income to capital (IP/LTCI): more difficult — benefit payment is contingent on both human survival and continuance of incapacity.

Conversion terms calculation: insurer's reserve is a starting point for capital value of income stream; reserve may contain statutory prudence margins and is on cohort basis — not an individual medically-assessed estimate. Anti-selection risk: claimants expecting short recovery take lump sum (worth more); those with long-term illness prefer income stream — increasing overall benefit payments.

key_terms.definition: An immediate annuity purchased on the open market to convert a lump sum (e.g. CI claim proceeds) into a regular income; not associated with a pension contract.
A PLA offered at enhanced (higher income) rates to reflect reduced life expectancy of the annuitant due to a serious medical condition following a CI claim.

Converting a stream of income benefit payments (e.g. under IP or LTCI) into an equivalent lump sum; more difficult than capital-to-income conversion as the benefit is contingent on both survival and continuance of incapacity.

Converting a lump sum benefit (e.g. CI proceeds) into a regular income stream, typically by purchasing a PLA; income level is unknown until claim time as it depends on age, term, annuity rates and health at claim.

exam_qa.q: Outline the main disadvantages of converting CI capital to income (from customer's perspective).
exam_qa.a: Customer will not know the level of income they will receive until the claim is paid, as it depends on: age of policyholder; term that income is to be paid; annuity rates available (dependent on interest rates, expected future mortality, competition, insurer's profit margin); state of health of the policyholder.

exam_qa.q: Explain the consequences of not allowing for each individual's state of health when calculating lump sum payments in lieu of income.
exam_qa.a: The insurer will suffer anti-selection: claimants who expect to recover shortly will take the lump sum (worth more than the income stream for a short claim); those with serious long-term illnesses will take the income stream. This will increase benefit payments over the whole portfolio.

subsections.title: Regulatory Requirements
bullets: Actuary must ensure product fits local regulatory environment: insurer authorised to write the class of business; appropriate sales routines and disclosure statements established.

In some territories, insurers cannot differentiate by gender in setting premium rates (EU from December 2012) — significant pricing implications; increased emphasis on target market, distribution channel and literature design to control mix of insured lives.
There may be direct restrictions on level of premium rates or charges for health insurance products.
Capital requirements depend on regulation (Solvency II framework); way supervisory solvency capital is calculated is specified in regulations and depends on type of business.
Regulatory regimes continue to develop — changes in equality legislation, capital requirement levels and sales process regulation all impact products and must be monitored carefully.

key_terms.definition: Insurance pricing that does not differentiate between male and female policyholders; mandatory in the EU from December 2012 following a European Court of Justice ruling.
EU regulatory framework for insurance companies specifying capital requirements, reserving standards and risk management requirements; uses a market-consistent valuation approach.

Capital required by the regulator (above policy reserves) to ensure insurers can meet obligations under adverse scenarios; method of calculation specified in regulations and varies by type of business.

subsections.title: ESG Considerations
bullets: As consumers become more aware of personal responsibility towards climate change/sustainability, they search for products designed with ESG factors in mind.

ESG factors: environmental (climate change, resource depletion, waste); social (human rights, modern slavery, working conditions); governance (bribery and corruption, executive pay, board diversity and structure).

Product design and pricing ESG issues: (i) offering appropriate ESG fund choices for unit-linked products; (ii) marketability risks if company is perceived as not meeting ESG standards (investment choices for non-linked assets, working practices, diversity, supplier ESG credentials).

Sustainable investment refers to investment approaches consistent with long-term sustainability of society and natural environment, relevant when investment is motivated by financial objectives with long-term sustainability regard.
Examples of sustainable investments: green energy companies (solar, wind, water); avoiding fossil fuels; green bonds; zero-emissions companies; fair-trade companies.
Increasing integration of ESG in investment practices across all insurance company types — due to risk/return and public interest as well as traditional ethical reasons.
Investment departments must screen assets and issuers, engage with investee companies (voting rights to encourage positive ESG practices); this increases costs passed on in pricing or fund charges.

Products should be designed in line with UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI) — an international organisation with six principles for incorporating ESG into investment analysis, ownership policies, disclosure, industry promotion, collaboration, and reporting.

key_terms.definition: Environmental, Social and Governance — a framework for evaluating the sustainability and ethical impact of an investment or business practice; increasingly integrated into insurance investment and product design.

Investment approaches that take account of ESG issues in a way consistent with the long-term sustainability of society and the natural environment, motivated by financial objectives with regard to long-term sustainability.
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals — 17 goals developed to promote global prosperity while protecting the environment; products should be designed in line with these objectives.

Principles for Responsible Investment — an international organisation dedicated to promoting ESG responsibility amongst investors; members commit to six principles covering ESG integration, active ownership, disclosure, industry promotion, collaboration and reporting.
Bonds issued to finance environmentally beneficial projects (e.g. renewable energy, energy efficiency); an example of a sustainable investment.

subsections.title: Removal of Barriers for Consumers
bullets: Care needed in product design to remove or minimise barriers for consumers — particularly for mental health conditions.

Measures: signposting customers declined cover to specialist brokers/support services; training claims staff to identify and compassionately deal with vulnerable customers; identifying underwriting process elements causing anxiety and changing them; bundling products with support services for mild mental health symptoms; providing rehabilitation services where appropriate.

subsections.title: Administration
bullets: Product launch requires expertise and guidance across: systems development; sales; underwriting; claims; customer service.

Administrative and claims systems must be fully operational from launch date — ideally tested and in place well before launch. Failures have caused missed opportunities, product failures, expense overruns and systems overloads.
Key staff must be recruited and trained before launch — not all staff suit the unique requirements of health insurance.
Even redesigning an existing product requires amending systems and retraining staff.

subsections.title: Expenses
bullets: Actuary must be aware of all expenses associated with a product and the contribution that expense allowances in pricing make towards covering them.
More detail on allowing for expenses in pricing models covered in SP1.

subsections.title: Lapses
bullets: Lapse rate assumptions in pricing basis depend on measures taken to control lapse rates, how historical experience is measured, and data used as starting point.
A company's own lapse rates are highly dependent on its distribution methods and after-sales service — statistics from another provider are at best a short-term measure.

Other key lapse determinants: level of economic confidence; ongoing competitiveness of the product. Own-product lapse experience should be calculated as early as possible, divided by distribution type if necessary.

Lapse and re-entry: in competitive markets, premium cutting can lead policyholders to lapse and take out a new policy at lower premium — most likely early in policy duration; only healthy lives can do this, worsening morbidity experience for remaining portfolio.

Early lapse of long-term policies almost invariably causes a loss (negative asset share); replacement policy incurs a further round of commission and expenses; worst case is repeated lapse and re-entry over several years.

Measures to reduce lapse and re-entry losses: introduce/increase premium reviewability; reduce initial and increase renewal commission; increase clawback of initial commission on early lapse; reduce initial commission for re-entrants who lapsed within 12 months either side; reduce (but not eliminate) underwriting requirements for new policy.

key_terms.definition: The practice where policyholders (especially on long-term contracts) lapse their existing policy and take out a new one with a lower premium due to competitive market price reductions; causes losses as early lapse has negative asset share and a new round of expenses is incurred.
Recovery by the insurer of initial commission paid to a distributor if the policyholder lapses the policy within a specified period; a tool to reduce lapse-and-re-entry losses.

The tendency for healthier, lower-risk policyholders to lapse their policies (e.g. in response to premium increases), leaving a worse-than-average risk portfolio and worsening future claims experience.
The accumulated value of premiums paid by a policyholder, less claims, expenses and charges; negative asset share at early durations means early lapse causes a loss to the insurer.

8.summary:
Cost of capital
• Regulation imposes significant reserving and statutory capital requirements that lead to capital strain at policy issue. Capital requirements can be assessed using model offices with sensitivity tests.
• Product design and reinsurance can be used to minimise capital requirements.
• Cost of capital is the loss of return on shareholders' investments resulting from holding reserves. It should be allowed for in the pricing models, taking into account: competing demands on the use of capital; the returns required by the different capital providers; the returns being obtained from other products or ventures.
• If the cost of capital is ignored in pricing, a subsidy must be provided from other business.
• Increasingly companies are adopting a market-consistent approach to product pricing, more consistent with embedded values, but can lead to greater volatility in the pricing.

Risks and risk management
• Need to consider risks linked to the product design and pricing process: product substitution; too many sales (capital problems); sales failure; expense overruns; product design failure; product costing failure (parameter error).
• Estimated claim rates and volatility are influenced by the claims management system. It should deal with all claims administration and payments, and be fully integrated where there are large claim numbers (eg PMI). It can be off-the-shelf or belong to a third party.
• Other claims control factors include pre-authorisation of PMI claims, rehabilitation of IP insurance claimants and counselling of IP insurance claimants and families of LTCI claimants.
• Customers need to be made 'risk aware' to achieve marketing success.
• A balance is needed between the most relevant data for pricing and the most credible data.

Reinsurance
• Reinsurance is part of the company's risk control mechanism and can reduce capital requirements. Cost of reinsurance must be met from premiums.
• Risk appetite drives extent of reinsurance; arbitrage opportunities (tax and capital) can make reinsurance mutually beneficial.
• Reinsurers can assist with capital strain at launch (financial reinsurance) and provide technical assistance.

Investment policy
• Main aim: appropriate nature/term/currency match to liabilities, maximise return, subject to risk appetite.
• PMI: short-term, high liquidity needed, investment return has minimal impact; repricing annually.
• CI/IP/LTCI: long-term, investment return more significant (especially pre-funded LTCI).
• PMI claims inflation is complex (utilisation, technology, age, like-for-like); differs from retail price or earnings inflation.
• Investment return and inflation assumptions must be consistent.

Renewal process and options
• Long-term policies: non-cancellable, premium rates may be reviewed, policy conditions cannot be changed.
• Short-term policies: terms on renewal may be identical to those for new entrant with same risk characteristics; full experience rating or NCD may apply.
• Reviewability: allows insurer to alter premiums if portfolio claims experience differs from expected; reviewed typically every 5 years.
• Selective lapsing risk associated with reviews — some insurers cap annual increases to reduce this.
• Increase options: Type 1 (automatic, pre-ordained or costed); Type 2 (periodic option, no re-underwriting, adverse selection loading required).
• Converting capital to income (CI → PLA) and income to capital (IP/LTCI) — anti-selection must be allowed for.

Other considerations
• Regulatory: authorisation, gender-neutral pricing, capital requirements per Solvency II framework.
• ESG: sustainable investment options for unit-linked; marketability if ESG standards not met; align with UN SDGs and PRI principles.
• Administration: all systems and staff must be in place and tested before launch.
• Lapses: own experience preferred; lapse and re-entry from premium cutting is a particular risk for long-term products.

9.title: Taxation Environment
9.summary:
Overview
• Tax can be based on: profits; investment income less expenses; premium income.
• Other taxes (such as stamp duty or employment related taxes) may also be payable.
• Policyholders (or their dependents) may be taxed on benefits.
• The taxation environment can change over time and may impact: the amount and timing of tax payable by an insurer; the attractiveness of insurance products (and compared to equivalent products offered by other institutions); the design of insurance products; policyholders buying habits.

The UK tax environment

Long-term business
• Long-term protection business is taxed as PHI business provided: benefits are payable on accident or sickness as defined in the contract; the term is at least five years (or to retirement if less); the contract is (broadly) not cancellable by the insurer.
• The majority of CI insurance, IP insurance and LTCI are classed as long-term business.
• Post January 2013, insurers pay corporation tax on pre-tax trading profits of long-term business. The accounts should be prepared in accordance with relevant accounting rules.
• Before January 2013, some long-term protection business was taxed on an I-E basis.

Short-term business
• Insurers pay corporation tax on tradable profits for short-term business (such as PMI). This is taxable profits adjusted for: investment return (excluding dividend income) – taxed on a fair value basis; the underwriting result and technical provision; other items (depreciation / disallowable entertaining); overseas / foreign profit.
• The underwriting result is premiums less claims and expenses less increase in technical provisions.
• The technical provisions are calculated on an accounting basis and an actuarial opinion is required to confirm the amount is ‘not excessive’. Technical provisions can include: reported claims provisions with case estimates or statistical provisions; provision for unearned premiums on the most appropriate basis; provision for unexpired risk reserve on a statistical basis; provisions for claims handing expenses.
• Tax losses may be: carried back and set against the previous year’s profits; carried forward and set against future trading profits; surrendered to other companies in the same UK tax group and used to offset the taxable profits of those companies.
• For a mutual insurer writing short-term insurance, underwriting losses / profits from mutual trading are exempt from tax. Investment income is taxed independently. If they make an underwriting loss, it cannot be offset against investment profits.

Taxation of policyholders
• Tax of policyholders may depend on the type of business, who receives the benefit and who has paid the premiums.

UK policyholder taxation
• CI insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits of stand-alone policies are tax free; group policies – if the employer pays the premiums it is usually deductible as a business expense for the employer, the employee would be taxed on the premium as a ‘benefit in kind’.
• IP insurance: individual policies – no tax relief on premiums, benefits are tax-free if premiums are paid by the life insured; group policies – if premiums are paid by the employer for the employee classified as business expense for the employer and qualify for tax relief, if premiums are paid by the employee no tax relief. If benefits are paid to the employer, taxed as a trading receipt, if they are then passed to the employee as a salary income tax will be payable.
• LTCI – varies depending on whether pre-funded or immediate needs product.
• PMI: no tax on claims; premiums – insurance premium tax is payable, if paid by employer paid out of pre-tax income (ie available for tax relief) but if paid by policyholders paid from post-tax income.

10.title: Approaches to Regulation
10.summary:
Regulatory objectives
• Policyholder protection and financial stability.
• Changes driven by the economic environment, harmonization across jurisdictions and advances in risk management.
• Insurers need to comply with local regulations and any supra-national regulators.

Regulatory responsibilities

Regulators normally produce rulebooks setting out rules / principles. Additional guidance may also be provided. Regulation may cover:
• Authorisation – may be required for individual jurisdictions or granted as part of a wider economic area.
• Business standards – affect day-to-day business, may cover senior management systems and controls (eg compliance, risk management, outsourcing, regulatory processes) and market conduct (requirements to act honestly, fairly and professionally eg around communication with clients, information disclosure to customers, product information, complaints)
• Supervisory reporting – specify information and frequency of reporting, may include balance sheet, revenue account, capital requirements (and assumptions / model), details of new business, forward looking analysis, stress / scenario testing
• Monitoring – regular assessment visits, review of reports discussed at governance meetings, review of regulatory reports
• Valuation and capital requirements – rules / principles around the valuation of assets and liabilities, assumptions, reinsurance and capital requirements
• Fit and proper person – specify rules by which those in senior / statutory actuarial roles need to operate, eg Chief Actuary may be responsible for ensuring technical provisions, appropriate methodology / assumptions etc, requirements on the insurer to keep the actuary informed, pay due regard to their advice and provide them with adequate resources
• Actions taken by regulators – may be able to require insurer to remedy situation, impose fines, require reinsurance to be purchased, increase capital requirements etc, some groups may be viewed as too large to fail (global systematically important insurers, G-SIIs) are subject to enhanced supervision
• Transfer of surplus – regulations around how much an insurer can transfer out of insurance fund (eg to pay dividends), may require surplus to be held in liquid assets
• Transfer of liabilities between insurers – may require Court and regulatory approval, independent expert may review impact on policyholders (in terms of benefits and TCF), may require scheme to be publicised to policyholder / shareholders.

Other legislation and guidance

Professional guidance

The IFoA has issued the following Actuarial Profession Standards:
• APS L1 (‘Duties and responsibilities of life assurance actuaries’)
• APS L2 (‘The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003’)
• APS X1 – Applying Standards to Actuarial Work
• APS X2 – Review of Actuarial Work
• APS X3 – The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings
• APS QA1 – Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.

Consumer legislation – May be specific legislation around what is a fair contract to avoid a significant imbalance.

Equality legislation – Discrimination based on factors such as age, disability, gender and pregnancy may be unlawful. Can cause issues when estimating the mix of business.

Climate change – Regulators are working on regulations that aim to limit the impact of climate change on financial systems.

Regulatory approach by jurisdiction

United Kingdom
• Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) – responsible for the prudential regulation of insurers to provide policyholder protection and promote resilience to failure (but not prevent failure).
• Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) – responsible for market conduct within the insurance industry.

United States
• State regulation co-ordinated by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), sets out model laws to encourage uniformity of reporting, valuation and capital requirements across states.
• At a federal level: Federal Insurance Office – monitors all aspects of the insurance sectors, advises on important international and national insurance matters; Federal Reserve Board – consolidated oversight of systematically important insurers.

China
• China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission – covers establishment and ongoing business activities of banking and insurers, including enforcement against regulatory violations. Implemented changes around capital requirements, risk management and disclosure transparency through C-ROSS framework.

Australia
• Australian Prudential Regulation Authority – takes a risk-based approach to help identify areas of greatest risk to target in a cost effective manner. Developed Life and General Insurance Capital standards aligned to Solvency II in the EU.

South Africa
• Regulation split between the Prudential Authority and the Financial Sector Conduct Authority.
• The Solvency Assessment Management framework is a risk-based approach to prudential regulation.

11.title: Solvency II (1)
11.summary:
Overview
• The Solvency II framework comprises three pillars: Pillar 1: Quantitative requirements; Pillar 2: Qualitative requirements and supervisory review; Pillar 3: Reporting, disclosure and market discipline.

Pillar 2
• The Board has overall responsibility for ongoing compliance with Solvency II.
• All insurance companies required to have a: risk management function; actuarial function; compliance function; internal audit function.
• Insurers must carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA), requires: identification of all risks (including qualitative risks) and related risk management processes and controls; quantification of ability to continue to meet MCR and SCR, allowing for new business; considered for strategic decisions.

Pillar 3
• Results of the solvency calculation, ORSA and risk management processes disclosed to the supervisor in the: Regular Supervisory Report (RSR), submitted annually; Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRT), submitted annually (with a subset quarterly).
• Public Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) produced annually.

Group reporting
• Each insurance group must cover its overall group SCR and group solvency floor. Each insurance subsidiary needs to cover its own SCR and MCR.
• Group supervision normally carried out at the top level company within the EEA. Additional rules apply to subsidiaries and parents located in a ‘third country’, ie non-EEA, depending on whether the country is equivalent or not.

Impact on business culture and strategy
• Engagement with Solvency II important throughout the business, up to senior management and Board level.
• Solvency II affects almost all activities, including: risk management and capital allocation; product mix and product design; asset mix; corporate structure; management information; external disclosures.

12.title: Solvency II (2)
12.summary:
Valuation of assets
• Valued using a market-consistent approach, ie at market value where available and mark-to-model where not.
• Recoveries expected from reinsurance shown as an asset on the balance sheet.

Valuation of technical provisions
• Based on a market-consistent approach, represent the amount the insurer would pay to transfer its obligations immediately to another insurer.
• Best estimate liability (BEL) and a risk margin: BEL is the present value of expected future cashflows, discounted using a ‘risk-free’ yield curve (ie term-dependent rates); Discount rate may be increased by a matching adjustment or a volatility adjustment if prescribed conditions met; Risk margin is intended to increase the technical provision to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurer for them to take on the BEL. Determined using the ‘cost of capital’ method.
• For unit-linked, the unit and non-unit components must be unbundled.

SCR
• Value at Risk measure based on a 99.5% confidence level of the variation over one year of the amount of ‘basic own funds’.
• Can be calculated using either standard formula or internal model (or a mixture).

Standard formula
• The Basic SCR (BSCR) is calculated by considering different modules of risks: market (interest rate, equity, property, credit spread, currency and concentration); counterparty default; insurance (separately for life, health and non-life business); intangible assets.
• The health insurance risk module comprised of SLT health, non-SLT health and catastrophe.
• SCR calculated separately for each risk module, results are aggregated using a specified correlation matrix to give the BSCR.
• The overall SCR obtained by adding allowances for the operational risk module and the loss absorbing capacity of technical provisions and deferred taxes.

Internal models
• Internal model must pass the following tests to gain approval: use test; statistical quality standards; calibration standards; profit and loss attribution; validation standards; documentation standards.

MCR
• Defined as a simple factor-based linear formula targeted at a Value-at-Risk measure over one year with 85% confidence.
• Floor of 25% and cap of 45% of the SCR.

Supervisory intervention levels
• Increasingly severe supervisory authority actions taken as a company’s eligible capital falls below the SCR (first point of intervention) and approaches the MCR.
• MCR is the ultimate point of supervisory intervention, below which the company would lose its authorisation.

Quality of capital resources
• ‘Own funds’ refers to assets in excess of technical provisions, plus it includes any subordinated liabilities, split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.
• Restrictions placed on the quality of capital used to cover the MCR and SCR.

Data quality
• EIOPA has issued advice on ‘Standards for Data Quality’, highlights the importance of having good quality data for the valuation of technical provisions.

13.title: Other Approaches to Solvency Regulation
13.summary:
United Kingdom
• Currently follows the Solvency II regulations of the EU, unclear how this will change following the UK’s exit from the EU.

USA
• National system of state-based regulation. National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) assists regulators to protect consumers and maintain financial stability.
• Insurers are required to maintain prudent reserves, capital and surplus at all times.
• Risk-based capital (RBC) system – uses a standard formula to determine capital requirements, derived from annual statement, considers size and risk profile of the insurer. Regulators can take preventative and corrective measures where the RBS result is considered too low, it is not the full amount of capital an insurer would need to hold. Conservative reserves are also required.
• Solvency Modernisation Initiative (SMI) – review started in 2008 following financial crash.
• Principle-Based Reserving (PBR) – replaced formula-driven approaches, insurers hold the highest of prescribed net premiums reserve, single economic scenario deterministic reserve and a stochastic reserve.
• ORSA – insurers are required to analyse all reasonably foreseeable and relevant material risks that might impact its ability to meet policyholder obligations.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: approach to regulation; capital – RBC is a ‘weaker’ level of capital requirement than SCR under Solvency II; statutory reserving – US approach historically formula-based with margins in assumptions for prudence, changing going forward following PBR, Solvency II requires market-consistent estimate of liabilities plus an explicit risk margin.

China
• C-ROSS framework has three tiers: Pillar I – quantitative capital requirements including risk factors applied to premiums and reserves for risks such as insurance, market and credit; Pillar II – qualitative capital requirements composed of a rating to determine the level of any regulatory intervention, explicit assessment of insurer’s risk management processes (ORSA equivalent) and a series of liquidity risk indicators; Pillar III – risk disclosure and transparency.
• Key differences to Solvency II are: Asset valuation – accounting value for C-ROSS, mark to market for Solvency II; Discount rate – C-ROSS similar to Chinese GAAP liability valuation, Solvency II uses risk-free curve; Own solvency risk assessment – C-ROSS doesn’t allow internal models.

Australia
• Life and General Insurance Capital – intended to increase risk sensitivity and provide consistency, strong parallels to Solvency II (has provisional equivalence): required to hold capital to 99.5% VaR over 1 year, calculated using standard method or approved internal model; regulator (APRA) can apply supervisory adjustment if capital considered inappropriate; required to produce an individual capital adequacy assessment report (similar to ORSA).

South Africa
• Solvency Assessment and Management framework – risk-based solvency framework with 3 pillars, based on Solvency II adapted for South African market: Pillar 1 – quantitative regulatory requirements, capital requirements reflect 1-in-200 year loss event, can use standardised formula for approved internal model; Pillar 2 – qualitative requirements and rules on supervision; Pillar 3 – regulatory reporting and public disclosure.

Asia / Pacific
• Range of approaches taken from minimum levels of capital (eg Myanmar) to formula based approaches (eg India), risk-based capital (eg Indonesia) and economic capital and ORSA approaches (eg Australia and China).

Latin America
• Mexico adopted Solvency II-like framework.
• Brazil granted provisional equivalence.
• Chile developing risk-based capital approach modelled on Solvency II.
• Brazil and Chile both have ORSA equivalents.

14.title: Profit Reporting
14.summary:
IFRS
• The IFRS standards (produced by the IASB) are the most widely adopted accounting standards in the world. EU regulations require all listed companies to use them when preparing their consolidated financial statements.
• IFRS standards of particular interest to health and care insurers are: IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts; IFRS 9 Financial Instruments; IFRS 7 Financial Instruments: Disclosures.
• Principles-based, main principles are fair representation, going concern, accruals based, comparability, compliance, reporting period, understandability and offsetting.

IFRS 17 Insurance Contracts
• IFRS 17 took effect from 1 January 2023. Addresses criticisms of previous regime (IFRS 4).
• General Measurement Model (GMM) – has four building blocks: the fulfilment cashflows; the time value of money; the risk adjustment (RA); the contractual service margin (CSM)
• Variable Fee Approach (VFA) – may be used for contracts in which the cashflows vary in line with the value of a pool of assets (eg unit-linked, with-profits).

Accounting for financial instruments

Currently contained in IFRS 9.

US GAAP
• Set of generally accepted accounting principles adopted by the US Securities and Exchange Commission (‘SEC’). Developed by many different organisations with the primary body of guidance for the health and care insurance actuarial and accounting work being produced by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (‘FASB’).
• Principles-based, with some differences from and some similarities to IFRS.
• The main underlying principles are: fair presentation; going concern; compliance; historical cost; revenue recognition; matching; full disclosure; objectivity; consistency; offsetting.

Supplementary reporting
• Embedded value (EV) techniques are intended to measure a realistic, risk-adjusted, valuation of shareholder cashflows arising from in-force business and net assets. Profit then defined as the change in embedded value plus the profit transfer.
• The usefulness of supplementary EV reporting impaired by a lack of consistency and transparency in the methods and assumptions adopted by different insurance companies. Led to the development of the European Embedded Value (EEV) principles and Market Consistent Embedded Value (MCEV) principles.
• Under Solvency II, the basic liability (BEL) is determined on a best estimate basis and so there are no prudential margins to be released in the EV PVIF.
• May still be a PVIF component in relation to: profits expected beyond the Solvency II contract boundaries; any difference between the best estimate investment returns assumed in the EV projection basis and the discount rates used in the BEL (including any matching or volatility adjustment); the release of the risk margin, after allowing for the cost of holding it.
• Some health and care insurers in jurisdictions that have implemented Solvency II are no longer reporting on EV.

15.title: Professional Standards and Guidance
15.summary:
Principles of professional conduct
• Actuaries are expected to comply with any relevant professional standards or guidance set by the professional body to which they belong. Standards evolve over time to reflect the market, regulations and international actuarial standards.
• Typically principles-based and include codes of conduct and disciplinary procedures.

Professional Standards Framework – UK
• The Actuaries’ Code – sets out 6 core principles: integrity, competence and care, impartiality, compliance, speaking up and communication.
• Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs) – the IFoA has issued the following APSs: APS L1: Duties and Responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries; APS L2: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Communications by Actuaries) Regulations 2003; APS X1: Applying Standards to Actuarial Work; APS X2: Review of Actuarial Work; APS X3: The Actuary as an Expert in Legal Proceedings; APS G1: The Chief Actuary in Non-Life Insurance; APS QA1: Quality Assurance Scheme for Organisations.
• Guidance Notes – specific mandatory requirements members are expected to comply with.
• Other non-mandatory resource material – issued by the IFoA, include: Speaking up – a guide for actuaries; Speaking up – a guide for employers of actuaries; Conflicts of interest – a guide for actuaries; Ethical data science.
• Technical Actuarial Standards – principles-based and issued by the FRC, of relevance to Subject SA1 are: TAS 100 (Generic TAS): Principles for Actuarial Work; TAS 200: Insurance. The FRC has also developed a ‘Framework for FRC Technical Actuarial Standards’ describing the authority, scope and application of all TASs, and a glossary of terms.

International Actuarial Professional Standards
• Consistency in the approach to setting professional standards is influenced by the International Actuarial Association (IAA) and the Actuarial Association of Europe (AAE): IAA – worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies, develops model International Standards of Actuarial Practice (ISAPs), encourages member associations to adopt or have standards that are consistent with these; AAE – fulfils a similar role within Europe, also provides advice and opinions to organisations within the EU, develops European Standards of Actuarial Practice (ESAPs) similarly to ISAPs, has a ‘Code of Professional Conduct’ and requires member associations to have a code that reflects these requirements.

16.title: Capital Management

subsections.title: Chapter Overview
bullets: Capital requirements are considered from three viewpoints: the company's own (economic capital), the regulator's (regulatory capital), and the rating agencies' (rating agency capital). These are covered in Section 1.
It is not sufficient to look only at current capital availability — companies project solvency positions into the future for various reasons covered in Section 2.

Section 3 covers the sources of capital for health and care insurance companies and how available capital changes over time, including proprietary vs mutual companies and market-consistent valuations.
Section 4 discusses ongoing solvency assessment; Section 5 explores the inextricable link between capital, risk and economic value.
Section 6 covers methods of raising capital or reducing required capital (equity, subordinated debt, securitisation, reinsurance, derivatives, ALM).
This chapter includes some references to with-profits policies; detailed knowledge of these is not required for this course.

subsections.title: Perspectives on Capital and Types of Required Capital Assessment
bullets: Capital strength is assessed from the policyholder, regulator or shareholder perspective. Policyholders and regulators focus on meeting obligations in extreme circumstances; shareholders focus on maintaining a rating and writing new business.

The most onerous perspective varies: for some companies the shareholders require more capital than the regulator; for others the regulatory requirement is more onerous. This depends on the nature of in-force business and the company's risk appetite.

Two key concepts: required capital — the capital needed to support the business; and available capital — the capital available to meet that requirement. In health and care insurance the word 'capital' can mean either, so context is important.
Available capital = value of assets − value of liabilities. The definitions of both need to be clarified (e.g. BEL vs technical provisions under Solvency II).

For a proprietary company, available capital measures shareholders' capital currently invested. For a mutual, available capital supports the corporate entity and is not earmarked for distribution to current policyholders.
Required capital: the level of assets required in excess of liabilities so that policyholder claims can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.

Three types of required capital assessment: (1) Economic capital — an internal determination based on the company's risk profile, risk appetite and ongoing business strategy; (2) Regulatory capital — solvency requirements prescribed by regulators, defining asset/liability values and capital requirements; (3) Rating agency capital — the view of rating agencies, whose adequacy standards matter for companies wishing to achieve or maintain a particular credit rating.
All three approaches consider policyholder protection to some extent, as there is a link between meeting policyholder claims and the company's broader risk appetite.

Reasons capital is required — split into two categories: (1) Risk capital: to cover day-to-day risks including market risk, credit risk, insurance risk (e.g. mortality, persistency, expenses), operational risk (e.g. outsourcing, terrorism, systems failures), liquidity risk, and group risk; (2) Working capital: to support the ongoing business strategy — fund new business (including attracting intermediaries), fund overheads and organic expansion costs (hardware, software, product development, premises, overseas initiatives), and acquire other companies or blocks of business.

Capital needs can be exacerbated by: mis-selling compensation costs (e.g. PPI); enforced flat charging structures (cap on insurer charges); lower investment returns in low-inflation environments; adverse demographic changes (e.g. increasing longevity raising LTCI costs); implications of treating customers fairly (TCF); and changing distribution patterns (e.g. introducing internet sales).

Companies subject to Solvency II or similar regimes are required to look at all risks as part of the ORSA, making them increasingly aware of the capital employed and the need to manage it and the return on it more effectively.

key_terms.definition: The level of assets required in excess of liabilities so that policyholder claims (or shareholder requirements) can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.
The capital available to meet the required capital — equal to the value of assets minus the value of liabilities, with both values needing careful definition.
An internal determination of required capital based on the company's own risk profile, risk appetite and ongoing business strategy.
Solvency requirements prescribed by regulators, defining the regulatory value of assets, liabilities and associated capital requirements.
Capital adequacy as assessed by rating agencies; important for companies wishing to achieve or maintain a particular credit rating.
Capital required to cover the day-to-day risks a company faces: market, credit, insurance, operational, liquidity and group risks.
Capital required to support the company's ongoing business strategy, including funding new business, organic expansion and acquisitions.

exam_qa.q: From what three perspectives is capital strength typically assessed, and what is each perspective primarily concerned with?
exam_qa.a: Policyholder and regulator perspectives: primarily concerned with the company's ability to meet its obligations in extreme circumstances. Shareholder perspective: concerned with whether the company has sufficient capital to target a particular credit rating and/or to continue writing new business.

exam_qa.q: Distinguish between risk capital and working capital.
exam_qa.a: Risk capital is capital required to cover day-to-day risks (market, credit, insurance, operational, liquidity, group). Working capital supports the ongoing business strategy: funding new business, overheads and development costs of organic expansion (hardware, software, product development, premises, overseas), and acquisitions of companies or blocks of business.

subsections.title: Why Solvency Projections Are Fundamental
bullets: There are several reasons why projecting solvency is fundamental to the successful management of a health and care insurer.
To understand and monitor the evolution of the company's risk profile over time.

To allow the impact of key business strategy decisions to be assessed — in particular, to show the amount of new business strain and hence the volume of new business that can be supported by available capital.
To estimate the pattern of capital releases, assess whether the company is achieving a suitable return on capital, and assess the cost of holding required capital when calculating the embedded value.

To meet statutory requirements — for example, Solvency II requires projections under Pillar 2 ORSA; projections of solvency capital are also needed to determine the risk margin under Pillar 1 and may form part of the SCR calculation if an internal model is used.

To support successful risk measurement and risk management more broadly — projections can be used with stress and reverse stress testing (starting from a known adverse outcome, such as breaching a regulatory capital requirement, and working out what events could cause it) to help companies understand their risks.

key_terms.definition: A Solvency II Pillar 2 requirement for insurers to regularly assess their own risk and solvency position, including forward-looking projections of solvency capital.
Starting from a known adverse outcome (e.g. breaching a regulatory capital requirement) and working backwards to identify what events could cause that outcome.
The initial capital cost of writing new business, reflecting the difference between premiums received and the upfront costs and reserves required.

exam_qa.q: List the main reasons why projecting solvency is fundamental to the successful management of a health and care insurance company.
exam_qa.a: (1) To understand and monitor the evolution of the company's risk profile. (2) To assess the impact of key business strategy decisions, including the volume of new business supportable. (3) To estimate the pattern of capital releases and assess return on capital / cost of holding capital for embedded value purposes. (4) To meet statutory requirements (e.g. Solvency II ORSA, risk margin determination, internal model SCR). (5) To support broader risk measurement and management, including stress and reverse stress testing.

subsections.title: Sources of Available Capital
bullets: In an insurance context, available capital = value of assets − value of liabilities. The definition of both terms may differ between economic, regulatory and rating agency assessments (Section 4).

Proprietary companies: the nucleus of available capital is the initial capital injected by proprietors. Over time, available capital is augmented by: additional amounts subscribed by shareholders or invested via subordinated loan stock; profits retained within the company (including 100% of with-profits surplus to which shareholders are entitled). Available capital is depleted by: distributions to shareholders as dividends; losses incurred on without-profits business (shareholders bear 100% of any losses).

Mutual companies: available capital typically stems from: any initial (or occasionally subsequent) capital injections; consistent under-distribution of surplus in respect of past generations of with-profits policyholders (often not deliberate, but a result of imprecise historic calculation techniques).
For both company types it is important to recognise that capital availability can be viewed from both a regulatory and a realistic perspective.

Most mutual health and care insurers operate on an 'entity' basis: available capital belongs to the corporate entity, not to any particular generation of policyholders, and is therefore not earmarked for distribution to current policyholders.

In a mutual, the amount of available capital may vary over time due to smoothing: if asset values rise significantly but liabilities are smoothed upward only slowly, the resultant increase in available capital needs to be retained to support the reverse situation (when assets fall and liabilities are smoothed downward slowly).

key_terms.definition: The process by which a mutual organisation transfers to being a proprietary (shareholder-owned) company — by sale to another company or by floating on a stock exchange.

The approach by which available capital in a mutual is regarded as belonging to the corporate entity rather than to any specific generation of policyholders, and is therefore not earmarked for distribution.
Debt whose repayment is subordinated to policyholder claims; counts as available capital from a policyholder perspective (increases free assets) but is a liability from a shareholder perspective.

subsections.title: Assessing Available Capital — Market-Consistent Valuations
bullets: Techniques for assessing available capital have evolved; one commonly used measure of assets and liabilities is market value.

The traditional approach of valuing liabilities using an expected earned rate on assets can give advance credit for any risk premium included in the return, which may not be suitable. There must be consistency between asset and liability valuation methods.

The market value of assets can usually be directly observed, but liabilities must be estimated using market-consistent techniques. The difference between market value of assets and market-consistent value of liabilities is referred to as 'realistic capital'.

In a market-consistent valuation, liability cashflows (not usually traded) are valued in line with the traded assets they most closely resemble: e.g. a fixed liability due in ten years is valued in line with a ten-year zero-coupon bond; an embedded financial option is valued in line with the market price of a similar option.

Where no exact traded price exists, market-consistent valuations use option pricing techniques and interpolation/extrapolation from existing prices to arrive at a value in a way that ensures no arbitrage is introduced (switching between asset classes should not appear to create value).

Simplified and alternative approaches are used in practice to make the valuation process easier: certainty equivalent valuation, risk-neutral valuation, and state-price deflators (stochastic discount factors that allow values to be presented using 'real-world' expectations rather than the less familiar 'risk-neutral' expectations).

Market-consistent techniques appropriately allow for non-diversifiable (systematic) risks — market risk and credit risk — which vary with interest rates, equity levels, volatilities, credit spreads and defaults.

Insurance cashflows are also subject to non-market risks (e.g. mortality, lapse rates). The appropriate allowance when estimating market value of liabilities is a complex area; however, economic theory says the appropriate starting point is best estimate assumptions. A risk margin is then typically added (e.g. via Solvency II technical provisions) because anyone accepting such a liability will require compensation for the risk that mortality or lapses may be worse than expected.

Realistic capital is the best measure of available capital from both policyholder and shareholder perspectives: from the policyholder viewpoint it captures all net assets available to meet claims (using best estimate liabilities) and its distribution best measures downside risk exposure; from the shareholder viewpoint it defines the capital actually locked into the company.

key_terms.definition: The difference between market value of assets and market-consistent value of liabilities; regarded as the best measure of available capital from both policyholder and shareholder perspectives.
A valuation approach in which liability cashflows are valued in line with traded assets of similar characteristics, ensuring no arbitrage is introduced.
Valuation of a derivative or risky asset by discounting expected payoffs using a risk-neutral probability measure; introduced in earlier actuarial subjects.
Stochastic discount factors that allow values to be presented using real-world expectations rather than risk-neutral expectations; used as an alternative market-consistent valuation approach.

Risks that cannot be diversified away — e.g. market and credit risk (interest rate movements, equity levels, volatilities, credit spreads, defaults) — which are appropriately captured within market-consistent liability values.

exam_qa.q: Explain what is meant by a market-consistent valuation of liabilities.
exam_qa.a: Liability cashflows (which are not usually traded) are valued in line with the traded assets they most closely resemble. Where no exact traded price exists, option pricing techniques and interpolation/extrapolation from existing prices are used in a way that ensures no arbitrage is introduced. The result ensures that the choice of backing assets does not affect the value of the liability.

exam_qa.q: Why is realistic capital considered the best measure of available capital from a policyholder perspective?
exam_qa.a: (1) It captures the value of all net assets available to meet policyholder claims and includes a realistic value of all existing liabilities (best estimate of assets needed to cover claims). (2) It captures the interaction between asset and liability values and market movements, so its distribution best measures policyholder exposure to downside risk. (3) Requiring that policyholder claims be met with a given probability is equivalent to requiring that realistic capital is positive with the same probability — it is only when realistic capital is negative that shareholders would possibly allow the company to default.

subsections.title: Economic Capital
bullets: Capital requirements cannot be assessed adequately by only considering the current value of liabilities and available assets — health and care insurers operate in a volatile environment, particularly with respect to market risk, and must be able to meet liabilities in those circumstances.

The company also needs to fund its ongoing business strategy; this requires projections that allow for both the size and probability of downside risks and the capital needed to support the future new business strategy.

Economic capital: an internal assessment of the value of assets required in excess of liabilities, allowing for the company's ongoing business strategy, so that claims can be met with a high degree of certainty when they fall due.

Economic capital is calculated by determining realistic capital across a number of scenarios using two approaches: (1) Stochastic modelling — commonly used to assess market and credit risks by running realistic capital across a large number of real-world economic scenarios; can also be used for other risks where a distribution of outcomes is available (e.g. stochastic morbidity models for CI, multi-state models for IP). (2) Stress tests — where the distribution of a particular risk is less clearly understood, the impact is assessed by applying stress tests to realistic capital.
Interdependencies between the various risks must be assessed in order to determine the overall economic capital requirement.

Time horizon for economic capital: some companies consider a one-year period; others the full run-off of the in-force book. There is no single correct answer. If risks can be managed through capital markets (e.g. hedged), a shorter horizon is sensible; if risks cannot be hedged, a longer horizon may be preferred. Calibration of stresses/scenarios must use a consistent time horizon and time step frequency.
Under Solvency II, all companies must assess their economic capital through the ORSA. Insurance companies in many other jurisdictions outside Europe also produce an ORSA.

key_terms.definition: An internal assessment of assets required in excess of liabilities, allowing for the company's ongoing business strategy and all risks, so that claims can be met with a high degree of certainty.
Running the realistic capital calculation across a large number of real-world economic scenarios to assess the distribution of capital outcomes, particularly for market and credit risks.
Assessment of the impact of a particular risk on realistic capital when the distribution of outcomes is less well understood; applied where stochastic modelling is not feasible.

exam_qa.q: Describe the two approaches used to calculate economic capital.
exam_qa.a: (1) Stochastic modelling: realistic capital is calculated across a large number of real-world economic scenarios, commonly used for market and credit risks. Can also be used for other risks when a distribution of outcomes is available (e.g. stochastic morbidity models for CI or multi-state models for IP). (2) Stress tests: where the distribution of a particular risk is less clearly understood, the impact is assessed by applying stress tests to realistic capital. Interdependencies between risks must also be assessed.

subsections.title: Regulatory Capital
bullets: Regardless of the outcome of any economic capital calculation, a health and care insurer must be able to meet its regulatory capital requirements.

Despite the greater risk sensitivity of many modern regulatory regimes, regulatory capital calculations may target a different measure (and level) of security for policyholders than internal economic capital calculations. For example, internal calculations could allow for premiums beyond applicable contract boundaries.
Any regulatory requirement to undertake an ORSA may include a solvency capital projection which may be equivalent or similar to an internal economic capital assessment.

subsections.title: Rating Agency Capital
bullets: The financial strength rating obtained by a company can drive the cost of raising additional capital and can also affect the health and care market's willingness to do business with the company.
It is therefore important for health and care insurers to understand the capital adequacy standards used by the rating agencies.
In recent years, rating agency capital adequacy standards have moved to embrace economic capital measures and other internal assessments of capital requirements (e.g. the ORSA).

subsections.title: Modelling — Practical Considerations and Proxy Models
bullets: Some forms of solvency projection, particularly for regulatory purposes, can raise practical difficulties: in theory, the probability of insolvency requires stochastic calculations to assess regulatory balance sheets at future time steps within a stochastic real-world model of future investment and insurance conditions — so-called 'stochastic within stochastic' or 'nested calculations'.

The outer model projects what is expected to happen to assets and liabilities in the future (a real-world stochastic model projecting investment returns, interest rates, inflation, withdrawals, claims, etc.).
The inner model calculates the liability at any particular time (e.g. pricing an option or guarantee) and would usually be calibrated to be market-consistent.

Nested calculations can require, say, 10,000 inner simulations for each time step of each of 10,000 outer simulations — an enormous number of calculations. It is therefore often desirable to simplify.

Simplification techniques include: closed-form solutions to estimate the regulatory balance sheet (e.g. the Black-Scholes formula for options/guarantees rather than a stochastic inner model); and replicating portfolios (a type of proxy model where assets are chosen to closely replicate liability behaviour).

The number of model runs required for economic capital or Solvency II internal capital models presents additional problems; it is often not computationally feasible to run the full model as many times as needed for all required scenarios. Short timescales for Solvency II reporting compound this.

Therefore, in recent years, several different types of 'proxy model' (also called 'light models') have been developed to allow much quicker and more frequent model runs for high-level decision-making or analysis.

Proxy model techniques include: polynomial or curve-fitting approaches; kriging (a form of multi-dimensional interpolation modelled by Gaussian processes); and replicating portfolio approaches. All are designed to be a simplification of the full ('heavy') model, reproducing its results to within an acceptable degree of approximation.

The replicating portfolio approach selects assets that closely replicate the behaviour of the liabilities, making those assets easier to model. However, this approach is becoming less popular as it can be difficult to find assets that replicate sufficiently well, e.g. due to non-market risks such as morbidity/longevity.

Polynomial proxy model example: using two risk factors x and y, liabilities can be modelled as f(x) = a₁x + a₂x² + a₃y + a₄y² + a₅xy + a₆x²y + a₇xy² + a₈x²y². In practice models include 10–20 risk factors and up to fourth-order terms. Risk factors might include interest rates, equity index level, index volatility, inflation, morbidity rates, etc.

Calibration process for proxy models: fitting scenarios are generated (using expert judgement), the heavy model is run under these scenarios, and the proxy model is calibrated to provide an acceptable fit (e.g. using least squares regression or another optimisation algorithm).

Validation: performed using a set of out-of-sample scenarios, comparing proxy and heavy model results. It is particularly important to obtain a good fit in the tails of the distribution (relevant for SCR or other high-confidence VaR measures).

Overfitting risk: if the heavy model is subject to simulation error, care must be taken not to overfit the proxy model (using too many factors and parameters), as this makes out-of-sample testing likely to be poor. Re-calibration is required periodically as the goodness of fit gradually deteriorates over time.

key_terms.definition: A modelling approach requiring stochastic inner simulations (market-consistent, to value liabilities at a future point) nested within each step of a stochastic outer simulation (real-world, to project future conditions).
The real-world stochastic model used to project future investment and insurance conditions (returns, rates, inflation, claims, withdrawals, etc.).
The market-consistent stochastic model used to calculate the value of liabilities (e.g. cost of options or guarantees) at a future point in time within the outer model.

A simplified approximation of the full ('heavy') actuarial model, designed to reproduce the heavy model results to an acceptable degree of accuracy but much faster, enabling high-frequency model runs.
A type of proxy model where assets are chosen to closely replicate the behaviour of the liabilities, making future modelling of those liabilities easier.
A form of multi-dimensional interpolation modelled by Gaussian processes, used as a proxy model technique for approximating the results of a heavy actuarial model.

exam_qa.q: Explain why 'nested calculations' present practical difficulties in solvency projection modelling.
exam_qa.a: To calculate the probability of insolvency under a regulatory basis, stochastic calculations are required to assess the regulatory balance sheet at future time steps within a stochastic real-world (outer) model. However, calculating the liability at each future time step may itself require a stochastic (market-consistent inner) simulation. This leads to a requirement for, say, 10,000 inner simulations for each time step of each of 10,000 outer simulations — an enormous number of calculations that is often not computationally feasible.

exam_qa.q: Describe how a polynomial proxy model is calibrated and validated.
exam_qa.a: Calibration: fitting scenarios are generated (using expert judgement), the heavy model is run under these scenarios, and the proxy model is fitted to the results using a least squares regression or other optimisation algorithm. Validation: a set of out-of-sample scenarios (not used in fitting) are run through both models and the results compared. A good fit in the tails of the distribution is particularly important if the SCR (or other high-confidence VaR) is being modelled.
